questions.text: Which 2 clinical features most strongly suggest a vesicovaginal fistula after laparoscopic hysterectomy? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Clear vaginal discharge 10 days post-op
B) Hematuria during immediate post-op voids
C) Normal cystoscopy at time of hysterectomy
D) Positive methylene blue dye test
E) Inability to void with suprapubic fullness

questions.explanation: Persistent watery vaginal discharge plus a positive dye test are classic for vesicovaginal fistula.

questions.text: Which of the following are typical signs and management principles of postoperative ileus following laparoscopy? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: A) Absence of flatus beyond 72 hours
C) Bowel distension with air-fluid levels on upright X-ray
D) Early mobilization to prevent and treat
E) Use of semi-synthetic opioids to stimulate bowel function

questions.explanation: Ileus: delayed flatus, diffuse air–fluid levels, distension; management emphasizes early mobilization and supportive care.

questions.text: Which statements about port-site hernia prevention are incorrect? Select all that are incorrect.

answerOptions.text: A) All 5 mm ports require fascial closure
B) 12 mm cutting trocars have higher hernia risk than dilating trocars
C) Midline incisions are at higher risk than lateral sites
D) Fascial closure is recommended for 10 mm umbilical ports
E) Specimen extraction should be done through stretched 5 mm ports to reduce defects

questions.explanation: 5 mm ports generally do not need closure; stretching small ports increases hernia risk.

questions.text: Which of the following are known risk factors for vaginal cuff dehiscence after laparoscopic hysterectomy? Select all that apply.

answerOptions.text: C) Use of delayed-absorbable barbed suture

questions.explanation: Thermal injury, smoking, early vaginal activity, and inadequate closure increase dehiscence risk.

questions.text: Which 3 nerves are most commonly injured from improper lithotomy positioning during gynecologic laparoscopy? Select 3 best answers.
questions.explanation: Peroneal (compression at fibular head), femoral (hip flexion/abduction), and lateral femoral cutaneous (inguinal ligament compression) are most common.

questions.text: A 42-year-old G0 underwent laparoscopic excision of endometriosis and total hysterectomy seven days ago. She reports constant leakage of urine that worsens with standing despite no prior history of urinary incontinence. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Constant watery leakage after hysterectomy is classic for vesicovaginal fistula.

questions.text: What is the least helpful test to confirm the presence of a vesicovaginal fistula?

answerOptions.text: A) Serum and vaginal fluid creatinine

questions.explanation: UA does not localize a fistula; dye testing and imaging/endoscopy are more diagnostic.

questions.text: When is surgical repair of a 3 cm vesicovaginal fistula ideally performed?

answerOptions.text: A) More than 48 hours after hysterectomy
B) Within 72 hours of symptom onset
C) After 3–6 months, if the diagnosis is delayed
D) Only after conservative management with catheter fails
E) After two or more episodes of urinary tract infection

questions.explanation: If not recognized immediately, repair is delayed 3–6 months to allow inflammation to subside.

questions.text: A 49-year-old G0 diabetic underwent laparoscopic hysterectomy and stage IV endometriosis excision three days ago. She now reports abdominal distension, nausea, vomiting, and no flatus. She denies severe pain or fever. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: POD 3 with distension, N/V, absent flatus and no peritoneal signs favors ileus.

questions.text: What is the most appropriate next step in management for a patient with suspected post-operative ileus?

answerOptions.text: C) NPO status and IV fluids

questions.explanation: Supportive care: bowel rest, IV hydration, electrolytes, mobilization.

questions.text: What is a common radiographic finding in a patient with ileus?

answerOptions.text: B) Free air in the mesentery
C) Dilated bowel with multiple air-fluid levels throughout GI tract
D) Isolated large bowel dilation with collapsed small bowel

questions.explanation: Ileus typically shows diffuse dilation and multiple air–fluid levels, including colon/rectum.

questions.text: Which laparoscopic port site always requires fascial closure, regardless of size?

answerOptions.text: A) 5 mm port placed with a cutting trocar
B) 8 mm port placed with a dilating trocar
D) 8 mm AirSeal port placed at Palmer’s Point

questions.explanation: Any site stretched for extraction should be closed due to enlarged fascial defect.

questions.text: Which factor increases the risk of port site hernia formation?

answerOptions.text: C) Trocar placed at a 90-degree angle to the fascia
D) Fascial stretching during specimen removal
E) Trocar placed lateral to the rectus muscle

questions.explanation: Stretching enlarges the defect and increases hernia risk.

questions.text: What is the approximate incidence of port site hernia after laparoscopy?
questions.explanation: Reported rates range roughly 0.2–3.2%, depending on port size, site, and closure.

questions.text: A 44-year-old G2P2 presents with sudden pelvic pain and vaginal bleeding six weeks after total laparoscopic hysterectomy, following intercourse. What is the most appropriate next step?

answerOptions.text: A) Schedule an office visit the next day
B) Reassure the patient this is normal
C) Advise ibuprofen and monitor at home
D) Immediate evaluation in the emergency department

questions.explanation: Concern for cuff dehiscence/evisceration warrants emergent evaluation.

questions.text: Which surgical technique reduces the risk of vaginal cuff dehiscence?

answerOptions.text: A) Use of electrosurgery to seal the cuff
B) Leaving the vaginal cuff open to drain
C) Reapproximation using delayed absorbable monofilament suture
D) Closing the cuff in one layer only
E) Closing with permanent braided suture

questions.explanation: Full-thickness closure with delayed-absorbable monofilament (or similar) lowers risk.

questions.text: What is a common presenting symptom of vaginal cuff dehiscence?

answerOptions.text: C) Sudden onset of pelvic pressure or bleeding

questions.explanation: Many patients report acute bleeding/pressure; evisceration may be visible.

questions.text: A 40-year-old patient has decreased sensation over her medial thigh and difficulty adducting her hip after laparoscopic hysterectomy. What nerve is most likely injured?
questions.explanation: Obturator nerve injury causes medial thigh sensory loss and weak hip adduction (note: clinical deficits are classically with adduction).

questions.text: Which of the following nerves is most commonly injured due to improper use of self-retaining retractors during pelvic surgery?
questions.explanation: Femoral nerve can be compressed by retractors or malpositioned blades against the psoas/iliacus.

questions.text: What is the most likely mechanism of pudendal nerve injury during pelvic surgery?

answerOptions.text: A) Compression between sacrum and retractor
C) Stretch injury during uterine manipulation
D) Entrapment during sacrospinous ligament fixation

questions.explanation: Pudendal nerve can be injured by entrapment during sacrospinous fixation, leading to perineal pain.
